questionText: A particle moves in the $x$-$y$ plane so that, for $t \ge 0$, its position is $x = t + 1$ and $y = t^{2} - 2t$. (a) Show that the particle is never moving vertically, and find the time and coordinates at which it is momentarily moving horizontally. (b) Find the Cartesian equation of the path, identify it, and confirm the point from part (a) is its vertex. (c) Find the speed of the particle when $t = 3$. Sketch the path.

questionDiagram.caption: The parabolic path with its lowest point marked.
questionDiagram.alt: A parabola rising to the right from about (1,0), dipping to a lowest point at (2,-1), then climbing steeply through (3,0) up to (5,8).

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: The horizontal velocity tells us how the $x$-coordinate changes. Here it is a constant $1$, meaning the particle always drifts to the right.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: The vertical velocity shows how the height changes. It is zero at one instant and changes sign, which is the clue to the turning behaviour.
Moving vertically would require the horizontal velocity to be zero. Since $\dot{x}$ is always $1$, the particle never moves straight up or down.
Moving horizontally means the height is momentarily unchanging, so we set the vertical velocity to zero.
The particle moves horizontally only at $t = 1$ s, which lies in the allowed range $t \ge 0$.
Substituting $t = 1$ into both equations gives the point $(2,\,-1)$ where the motion is momentarily horizontal.

steps.description: Eliminate t for the Cartesian form
steps.explanation: To find the shape of the path we remove the parameter, rearranging the simpler equation for $t$.
Replacing $t$ in $y$ with $x - 1$ expresses the height purely in terms of $x$.
Multiplying out and collecting terms gives $y = x^{2} - 4x + 3$, an upward-opening parabola.
The Cartesian parabola has its minimum where $x = 2$, giving $y = -1$. This matches the horizontal-motion point $(2,\,-1)$, confirming it is the vertex.
The speed needs both velocity components, so we evaluate them at $t = 3$.
Speed is the magnitude of the velocity vector, combining horizontal and vertical parts with Pythagoras.
Squaring and adding gives $17$ under the root, so the exact speed is $\sqrt{17}$.
As a decimal the speed at $t = 3$ is about $4.12$ units per second.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The particle never moves vertically (since $\dot{x} = 1 \neq 0$); it moves horizontally only at $t = 1$ s, at the point $(2,\,-1)$. The Cartesian path is $y = x^{2} - 4x + 3$, an upward parabola whose vertex is exactly $(2,\,-1)$. At $t = 3$ the speed is $\sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$.
